name: Toro – tour around Villalonso and Fresno de la Ribera
name_original: Toro – vuelta por Villalonso y Fresno de la Ribera
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Toro, located in Spain's Castile and León region, traverse diverse landscapes. The area features varied terrain, from flat sections along riverbanks to routes with moderate elevation gains. Cyclists can explore paths alongside the Duero River and through agricultural areas, offering a mix of natural and cultural scenery. The region provides a network of routes suitable for different fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Toro The most popular road cycling route is Villalonso Castle and Morales de Toro Loop, a 25.4 miles (40.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 38 minutes to complete. This route offers an easy ride through the local countryside, passing by historical landmarks. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Toro – tour around Villalonso and Fresno de la Ribera, a moderate 45.8 miles (73.6 km) path. This route includes a greater distance and elevation, providing a more challenging experience through the region. Local road cyclists also love the Toro and Duero River Loop, a 10.4 miles (16.7 km) trail leading through riverine landscapes, often completed in about 45 minutes. This easy route offers scenic views along the Duero River. Road cycling around Toro is defined by routes along the Duero River, through agricultural plains, and past gentle hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more strenuous rides. The routes in Toro are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 260 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Toro's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Toro?
A: Road cycling routes around Toro offer a diverse landscape. You'll find flat sections, particularly along the Duero River, as well as routes with moderate elevation gains. The terrain often includes agricultural plains and gentle hills, providing a varied experience for cyclists.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Toro area?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes available around Toro, catering to various skill levels. This includes a mix of easy and moderate rides, ensuring options for most cyclists.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or casual rides?
A: Yes, Toro offers several easy road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1370229904/villalonso-castle-and-morales-de-toro-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villalonso Castle and Morales de Toro Loop&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 40.8 km ride through the countryside. Another accessible option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1023165272/toro-and-duero-river-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toro and Duero River Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a scenic 16.7 km trail along the Duero River.

Q: Are there more challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: While Toro doesn't feature routes classified as 'difficult' in our current selection, there are moderate options that offer a greater challenge. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025516077/toro-tour-around-villalonso-and-fresno-de-la-ribera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toro – tour around Villalonso and Fresno de la Ribera&lt;/a&gt;, for example, is a 73.6 km path with significant elevation gain, providing a more strenuous experience.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Toro?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes in Toro are designed as loops. Popular circular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1370229904/villalonso-castle-and-morales-de-toro-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villalonso Castle and Morales de Toro Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1023165272/toro-and-duero-river-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toro and Duero River Loop&lt;/a&gt;, both offering convenient starting and ending points.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer scenic views of the Duero River and traverse the natural reserve of Las Riberas de Castronuño-Vega del Duero. You can also pass by cultural landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5268792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5450630" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Clock Tower of Toro&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5623269" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Duero River Meander from La Muela Park&lt;/a&gt; is another notable viewpoint.

Q: Are there routes that follow the Duero River?
A: Absolutely. The Duero River is a prominent feature of the region's cycling landscape. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1023165272/toro-and-duero-river-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Toro and Duero River Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, offering picturesque views along the riverbanks. Other routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38149700" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Clock Tower of Toro – Duero River loop from Rural Hotel&lt;/a&gt;, also incorporate sections along the river.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Toro?
A: The road cycling routes in Toro are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, scenic river views, and the mix of natural and cultural sights along the paths.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Toro?
A: The Castile and León region, where Toro is located, generally offers favorable cycling conditions during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summer can be quite warm, while winter might present colder conditions, though options for year-round cycling exist.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by historical castles or significant buildings?
A: Yes, several routes offer glimpses of historical architecture. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1370229904/villalonso-castle-and-morales-de-toro-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villalonso Castle and Morales de Toro Loop&lt;/a&gt; passes by historical landmarks, including the Villalonso Castle. You can also find routes that take you past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5268792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of Santa María la Mayor&lt;/a&gt; in Toro.

Q: Are there any routes that connect Toro with other towns or cities?
A: Yes, some routes extend beyond Toro, connecting to nearby towns. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39836795" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de los Poetas – Castle of Zamora loop from Toro&lt;/a&gt; offers a longer ride that takes you towards Zamora, providing a broader exploration of the region.
tips: The small town of Morales de Toro is in the plains located to the east of Toro at an altitude of 700m. Dating back to Roman times, the city was controlled by both Muslims and Christians during the Middle Ages.

The current castle was built in the 15th century on the site of an earlier 13th-century fortification, which belonged to the Order of Alcántara.

In 1470, Juan de Ulloa and his wife, María de Sarmiento, rebuilt it in a palatial style, placing their coats of arms above the entrance. Juan supported the Portuguese cause in the War of the Castilian Succession and died defending the castle in 1476; his widow then defended it for a time.
Small rural town on the banks of the Duero River next to the N-122 national highway that connects Zamora with Tordesillas. Beautiful views of the Douro River landscape.
From this incredible viewpoint, near Alcázar del Toro, there are spectacular views of the Duero Valley and the surrounding landscape, especially the Puente Mayor, a medieval stone structure.

The Alcázar of Toro is a historic fortification located in the town of Toro (Zamora, Castile and León), with great heritage and scenic value.

It was built in the 10th century during the Christian repopulation, as part of the defensive system against Muslim incursions.
It was reinforced in subsequent centuries, especially in the Middle Ages, when Toro acquired strategic importance in the Kingdom of León and later in Castile.
It played a prominent role in the War of the Castilian Succession (1475), in the famous Battle of Toro between supporters of Isabella I of Castile and Joanna la Beltraneja.

The inspiration for the Collegiate Church came from the Cathedral of Zamora, and its design also influenced other buildings, such as the Old Cathedral of Salamanca. Construction began in the 12th century under the auspices of the kings of León. It is one of the most characteristic examples of Romanesque in its transitional phase. The works extended over an unusually long period, from the last third of the 12th century to the middle of the 13th. The collegiate church was built in two stages: the first included the side portals, walls and layouts, while the second focused on the roof and a dome with attached towers. If you ever have the opportunity to visit Toro, don't miss the opportunity to explore this wonderful collegiate church. It is a true historical treasure! 🌟

The Clock Tower, also known as the Clock Arch, is located in the walled area of Toro, in the province of Zamora, Spain. Its construction began in 1719 during the reign of Philip V and was completed nineteen years later. The design is attributed to architect Joaquín Churriguera. The tower is located above the old Market Gate, which dates back to the 15th century and surrounded the first walled area of the city. Its height allows it to be seen from any point in Toro. If you ever visit Toro, don't miss the opportunity to admire this historic tower and its clock. It is a true symbol of the city! 🕰️🌟
